quote: She's got a softball thing till seven till eight. Do you have anything on Wednesday?
stewart_read: Chris references a third party ('she') whose schedule is actively gating the appointment time. Carter never asks who 'she' is. The reactive read is the correct one here: Carter simply did not notice or did not act on a clear co-decision-maker signal. Three separate 'she' references across the call with zero acknowledgment from Carter makes any 'strategic deferral' hypothesis implausible — there is no later move Carter sets up, and the call ends without the identity ever being established.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter already knew from prior context who 'she' is and judged it unnecessary to ask. This is implausible: even if Carter had prior knowledge of a spouse, the schema requires confirming co-decision-maker presence on the appointment, not just accommodating their schedule.
coaching_implication: Pull this call in the 1-on-1 and stop it at 01:22. Ask Carter: 'Who is she?' Then ask: 'What does the schema say you do when a co-decision-maker's schedule is controlling the appointment?' The protocol is not to work around her schedule — it's to confirm she'll be on the call. Carter needs to hear the distinction between scheduling around someone and including them.

quote: Doesn't look like she got anything on her schedule.
stewart_read: Second touch of the same unidentified third party. Chris is now actively checking her calendar, which signals she has meaningful authority over this appointment. Carter's response is to treat this as a scheduling update only. The co-decision-maker identification window has now passed twice.
coaching_implication: This is the second missed window in the same pattern. In the 1-on-1, show Carter both timestamps back-to-back. The question to ask: 'At what point did you know someone else's schedule mattered here, and what should that have triggered?' The answer is protocols.spouse_decision — confirm she'll be on the call, not just that her calendar is clear.

quote: k. That should be fine. Cool. We'll do 5PM on Wednesday, and they'll come by. It'd be forty minutes. It shouldn't take too long at all.
stewart_read: Carter's close skips every required button-up element: no computer/no-driving requirement stated, no 'is that fair' verbal handshake, no text follow-up promise, and the duration stated ('forty minutes') is inconsistent with the schema's one-hour block requirement. Each skipped element is a documented no-show risk factor. This is not a strategic omission — there is no evidence Carter is deferring these to a follow-up text or a second touch.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may send a text after the call that covers some of these elements. Even if true, the verbal handshake ('is that fair') and the computer/no-driving anchor cannot be recovered in a text — they require a live confirmation moment.
coaching_implication: Walk Carter through the button_up checklist against this close verbatim. Five elements required; zero delivered. Frame it as a paycheck-protection issue: a sit that no-shows doesn't pay. The 'is that fair' handshake and the computer anchor are the two highest-leverage items to drill first.

stewart_read: Carter's phrasing implies an in-person visit ('come by'), not a phone appointment. The schema requires the phone appointment format to be explicitly stated. If Chris is expecting someone to show up at his door on Wednesday at 5PM and the closer calls instead, the sit is at immediate risk of confusion or refusal. This is a standalone sit-risk distinct from the broader button-up gap.
alternative_hypothesis: Ion's closer appointments in this market may actually be in-person. If so, 'they'll come by' is accurate. However, the schema specifies phone_appointment_specified as a required button-up element, and Carter made no explicit statement of format either way — the ambiguity itself is the problem.
coaching_implication: Ask Carter directly: 'Is this a phone appointment or an in-person visit?' If phone, Carter needs to say so explicitly every time. If in-person, the schema's phone_appointment_specified element needs to be reconciled with Ion's actual closer format for this market. Either way, the ambiguity needs to be resolved before Wednesday.

quote: Thank you very much. Of course. Yep. We'll see you then. Yeah. Bye.
stewart_read: The call ends with no mention of a follow-up text. The schema requires the setter to inform the customer that a text with appointment details will follow. The text is also the only mechanism for a personalized tieback (schema: text_after_call.required_elements). Without the promise, the customer has no written confirmation of the Wednesday 5PM appointment — increasing no-show risk on an already fragile booking.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may send the text without having promised it on the call. A text sent without a promise is better than no text, but the schema's intent is that the customer expects it — the expectation itself increases show rate.
coaching_implication: Add 'I'll send you a text right after this with the details' as a non-negotiable close line. Pair it with the text_after_call template. For this specific appointment, confirm Carter sent the text and that it included a tieback — even a minimal one ('looking forward to walking through the solar estimate for your home').

quote: I'll be super quick. I'm just calling because we built that solar estimate for your home, but just haven't been able to get that out to you yet.
stewart_read: Carter references a solar estimate but makes no attempt to surface bill pain, anchor the customer's current cost, or create any emotional alignment against the utility. This is a recycled or prior-touch lead — the estimate already exists — which makes the bill anchor even more accessible: Carter could have said 'we built this around your usage, and I want to make sure the numbers still reflect what you're paying.' Instead, the call is purely logistical.
alternative_hypothesis: On a rescheduling call, bill anchoring may be out of scope — the qualification work was done on the prior touch. Strategic read: Carter is correctly holding setter scope and not re-qualifying a lead that's already in the pipeline. This is the more defensible hypothesis given the call context.
coaching_implication: This is a lower-priority gap given the rescheduling context. However, if Carter is routinely treating all rescheduling calls as pure logistics, flag it: even a single sentence reconnecting the customer to their bill pain ('we want to make sure the design still matches what you're seeing on your bill') takes 10 seconds and meaningfully increases show rate on a fragile booking.

quote: How long do you think we'll be?
stewart_read: Chris asks directly how long the appointment will take — a time-constraint protocol trigger. Carter answers '40 minutes,' which is inconsistent with the schema's one-hour block requirement (button_up.one_hour_block_stated). The schema's one-hour framing exists to set expectations that prevent the customer from cutting the closer short. Carter's '40 minutes' answer undersells the time commitment and may create a conflict when the closer needs the full hour.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may have accurate knowledge that Ion's closer appointments in this market run 40 minutes, not 60. If so, the schema's one-hour block element needs to be reconciled with actual closer duration. Until that's confirmed, the discrepancy is a coaching gap.
coaching_implication: Confirm with management: what is the actual expected duration for closer appointments? If it's 60 minutes, Carter needs to say 'about an hour' consistently. If it's 40 minutes, the schema's one_hour_block_stated element needs to be updated. Either way, Carter should not be giving a duration that conflicts with what the closer will need.

quote: Just do 05:00 on Wednesday. There's nothing on her schedule, and I don't have anything on mine. So
stewart_read: Chris books the appointment contingent on 'her' schedule being clear — and 'her' identity has never been established. The booking is real but structurally conditional: it holds only if the unidentified third party remains available and, if she is a co-decision-maker, only if she is present. Carter accepts the booking without surfacing this dependency.
coaching_implication: Flag this in the handoff brief for the closer. The closer needs to know: (1) an unidentified third party's schedule was the gating factor for this time slot, (2) her role — spouse, co-title-holder, or other — was never confirmed, and (3) if she is a co-decision-maker and is not on the call, the closer should expect a 'I need to talk to her first' exit at the close.
